# Students per teacher

A natural question is how crowded Cambridge's classrooms are compared with
nearby districts. The measure most people picture is **class size** — the number
of students in a typical classroom — but NYSED does not publish class size by
district in the data used here. The closest available measure is **K-12 students
per teacher**: a district's K-12 enrollment divided by its number of teachers.

Students per teacher is a useful staffing comparison, but it is **not** class
size, and it is always lower than class size — because it counts *every* teacher
(including part-time teachers and specialists who don't lead a regular class),
not just the teachers standing in front of a full classroom. Read it as a
staffing ratio, not a count of children in a room.

A reminder from the [Overview](index.qmd): the teacher figure is a district-wide
*headcount* (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), so this is an approximate
measure for comparing districts and tracking change over time. Lower means fewer
students per teacher.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import os
import sys

# Make the shared helper importable regardless of Quarto's working directory.
sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())

import polars as pl
from plotnine import (
    aes,
    geom_line,
    geom_point,
    ggplot,
    labs,
    scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous,
)
from great_tables import GT, loc, md, style

import comparisons as cc
```

## Students per teacher, over time

The chart below tracks the graph group from school-year-ending 2018 (the first
year teacher counts exist) through 2025.

**Cambridge now has the fewest students per teacher of the group.**

```{python}
#| label: fig-students-per-teacher
#| fig-cap: "K–12 students per teacher by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25). Lower means fewer students per teacher. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases."
g = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)  # 5 districts, 2018+
    .select("district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="K–12 students per teacher",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="District K–12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount, 2017–18 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)
```

In 2017–18 only Salem had a lower ratio than Cambridge. Cambridge's ratio then
drifted down — its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% (from 869 to 741) while its
teacher headcount stayed roughly flat — and by the two most recent years
Cambridge has the **lowest** students-per-teacher ratio of the five. A jump in
reported teachers in 2023–24 pushed its 2024 figure especially low (8.2).
Schuylerville and Greenwich actually fell *more* over the full period (about
2.0 and 1.9, versus Cambridge's 1.5), but from much higher starting points, so
they remain above Cambridge.

## The full comparison group

The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are
school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2018 to 2025 (a
negative value means *fewer* students per teacher than in 2018). Cambridge is
highlighted.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-students-per-teacher
#| tbl-cap: "K–12 students per teacher by district and school year ending."
t = cc.panel_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP)

# Long -> wide (years become columns), Washington County districts first,
# then alphabetical within each region.
wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="k12_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns(
    (pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg")
)

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="K–12 students per teacher",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and "
            "Student &amp; Educator database (teacher counts). "
            "Ratio = district K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount; an "
            "approximate comparison measure, not official class size. "
            "Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)
```

Across the wider group, Cambridge ends among the lowest ratios — close to Salem
— while the rest of Washington County's K-12 districts span a wider range. The
table is also a reminder that these districts differ greatly in size (compare
small districts like Fort Edward with larger ones like Hudson Falls and
Granville), which shapes how much a single hire or a small enrollment swing
moves the ratio.

## A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE

The ratio above has two limitations worth testing. NYSED reports a teacher
*headcount*, whereas the standard pupil-teacher ratio — the one NCES, the OECD
and most cross-state comparisons use — divides enrollment by teacher
*full-time-equivalents (FTE)*. And it comes from a single state source. The
federal **Common Core of Data (CCD)** offers an independent check: it reports
teacher FTE and enrollment for every district. (CCD labels a school year by its
fall term, so its years are shifted here to match NYSED's school-year-ending
labels.)

First the diagnostic question — do the two measures agree for Cambridge?

**The two measures never differ by more than about two-thirds of a student per teacher, and both fall sharply in 2023–24.**

```{python}
#| label: fig-cambridge-sources
#| fig-cap: "Cambridge students per teacher: NYSED (teacher headcount) vs. NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE), by school year ending. Sources: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases; NCES CCD via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal."
ny = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)
    .select("year_end", pl.col("k12_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NYSED (teacher headcount)").alias("source"))
)
ccd_cam = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter((pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS) & (pl.col("year_end") >= 2018))
    .select("year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NCES (teacher FTE)").alias("source"))
)
both = pl.concat(
    [
        ny.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
        ccd_cam.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
    ]
)
source_colors = {
    "NYSED (teacher headcount)": "#C8102E",
    "NCES (teacher FTE)": "#2C6E9B",
}

(
    ggplot(both, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="source"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2)
    + scale_color_manual(values=source_colors, name="")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher",
        title="Cambridge students per teacher: headcount vs. FTE",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)
```

The two lines stay close because, in small districts, a teacher headcount and its
full-time-equivalent are nearly the same — there are few part-timers. More
important, the independent FTE measure shows the **same sharp decline in
2023–24**, so that turning point is not an artifact of how NYSED counts teachers.

Having confirmed the two agree, the next chart and table repeat the earlier views
using the NCES FTE-based ratio — the version directly comparable to other states
and to national figures.

**On the NCES measure too, Cambridge has the lowest ratio of the group in the two most recent years.**

```{python}
#| label: fig-students-per-teacher-ccd
#| fig-cap: "NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by school year ending. Source: NCES Common Core of Data via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal."
gc = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2018)
    .select("district", "year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge_ccd = gc.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(gc, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge_ccd, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher (NCES FTE)",
        title="District enrollment ÷ teacher FTE (NCES CCD), 2017–18 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-students-per-teacher-ccd
#| tbl-cap: "NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by district and school year ending."
tc = cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2018, 2025))

wide_c = (
    tc.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="ccd_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols_c = [c for c in wide_c.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide_c = wide_c.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide_c, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Students per teacher (NCES FTE basis)",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols_c + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols_c)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE and enrollment), "
            "via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal. Ratio = district "
            "enrollment ÷ teacher full-time-equivalents — the nationally "
            "standard pupil-teacher ratio. CCD years are shifted to "
            "school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)
```

One difference to keep in mind: the NCES ratio divides *total* district enrollment
(which includes pre-K) by teacher FTE, while the NYSED ratio earlier uses K-12
enrollment and a headcount. The two therefore differ slightly in level, but they
agree on the pattern — which is what the comparison turns on.
